regimens.notes: Liposomal Doxorubicin is preferred for first-line treatment of advanced Kaposi sarcoma in non-transplant patients. Due to potential risk of cardiotoxicity, perform echocardiogram prior to initial course and repeat periodically. Consider limiting lifetime dose per prescribing guidelines, but patients who need continued treatment may be safely treated beyond 1000 mg/m2 [121,122,125-129]. Corticosteroid pre-medication should generally be avoided [KS-F footnote b].

trial_data.or_other_data: ORR 56% for Paclitaxel vs 46% for Pegylated-liposomal Doxorubicin (P = .49); median PFS 17.5 vs 12.2 months (P = .66); 2-year survival 79% vs 78% (P = .75) [133]

regimens.notes: Paclitaxel is an alternative first-line option for advanced Kaposi sarcoma in non-transplant patients. Dose schedule options include weekly, biweekly, or every 3 weeks dosing. Neutropenia is the most frequent dose-limiting toxicity [130-133].

drugs.name: Sirolimus
drugs.schedule: loading dose followed by 0.04–0.06 mg/kg/day to maintain trough blood levels of 6–10 ng/mL or 2 mg PO daily

trial_data.or_other_data: All cutaneous lesions disappeared with histologic remission at 6 months; no acute rejection or changes in kidney-graft function [21]

regimens.notes: Sirolimus is preferred for transplant-associated Kaposi sarcoma. Switching to Sirolimus for immunosuppression may be sufficient for KS control and treatment, especially if able to halt or reduce other immunosuppressive agents. For PWH on ART, consult with an ID pharmacist prior to dosing due to drug-drug interactions [KS-F footnote e].

trial_data.or_other_data: ORR 40% (95% CI, 16.3–64.7); median duration of response 12.5 months [143]

regimens.notes: Nivolumab is an option for relapsed or refractory Kaposi sarcoma. It should not be used in patients with MCD or KICS due to risk of flare. If history of KSHV-associated diseases, use with caution and more frequent monitoring [KS-F footnote i]. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted [KS-F footnote k].

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.schedule: every 3 weeks for up to 6 months

trial_data.or_other_data: Best overall response rate 71% (95% CI, 44–90); CR 22%, PR 59%, SD 29% [144]

regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab is an option for relapsed or refractory Kaposi sarcoma. It should not be used in patients with MCD or KICS due to risk of flare. If history of KSHV-associated diseases, use with caution [KS-F footnote i].

trial_data.or_other_data: ORR 60% in PWH group (95% CI, 32%–84%) [72]

regimens.notes: Pomalidomide has accelerated FDA approval for HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma after ART proves insufficient and for patients without HIV. Patients of childbearing potential require effective contraception per REMS program [KS-F footnote l]. The NCCN Panel believes the 4-mg dose is sufficient [KS-F footnote m].

drugs.name: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel
drugs.schedule: on days 1, 8, and 15 of each 28-day cycle

trial_data.or_other_data: All patients had partial or complete responses [148]

regimens.notes: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel may be used if Paclitaxel intolerant. Evidence is limited to a small phase II trial and case reports [148,149].

drugs.name: Bortezomib
drugs.schedule: on days 1, 8, and 15 of each 28-day cycle

trial_data.or_other_data: PR rate 60% in evaluable patients; 83% in 1.6 mg/m2 cohort [150]

regimens.notes: Bortezomib has activity in relapsed/refractory HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma. Maximum tolerated dose was not reached in the pilot trial [150].

drugs.name: Etoposide
drugs.schedule: for 7 days of each 14-day cycle, escalate to 100 mg/day after 2 cycles if no response and no toxicity >Grade 2, and further to 150 mg/day based on tolerance and response

trial_data.or_other_data: No difference in response at 48 months between immediate vs as-needed etoposide [69]

regimens.notes: Oral Etoposide has activity in relapsed/refractory Kaposi sarcoma. The dose escalation schedule used in the A5264/AMC-067 trial is recommended [69,151-153].
Gemcitabine has activity in relapsed/refractory HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma [155,156].

trial_data.or_other_data: PR 33%, SD 20%; median duration of response 8 months [160]

regimens.notes: Imatinib has activity in HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma [158-160].

trial_data.or_other_data: ORR 87%; 6-month PFS rate 76.5% [161]

regimens.notes: Ipilimumab plus Nivolumab is an option for relapsed or refractory classical Kaposi sarcoma. Should not be used in patients with MCD or KICS [KS-F footnote i].
Lenalidomide has activity in relapsed/refractory HIV-related Kaposi sarcoma. Patients of childbearing potential require effective contraception per REMS program [KS-F footnote l], [71,73].

drugs.name: Sirolimus
drugs.schedule: loading dose followed by 0.04–0.06 mg/kg/day to maintain trough blood levels of 6–10 ng/mL or 2 mg PO daily

regimens.notes: Sirolimus may be used for relapsed or refractory transplant-associated Kaposi sarcoma. Consultation with ID pharmacist for PWH on ART is recommended [KS-F footnote e].

regimens.site: Kaposi sarcoma with immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS)

drugs.name: Thalidomide
drugs.schedule: orally, starting dose titrated to effect and tolerability

trial_data.or_other_data: PR 47%, SD 12%; median time to progression 7.3 months [162]

regimens.notes: Thalidomide can be considered for patients with Kaposi sarcoma and corticosteroid-refractory IRIS. It is an active agent against both KS and IRIS. Patients of childbearing potential require effective contraception per REMS program [KS-F footnote l], [70,162,163].

trial_data.or_other_data: CR 9%, PR 34%; median duration of response 6 months [164]

regimens.notes: Vinorelbine is an effective and safe drug for AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma [164].

This is the preferred regimen for KICS. Liposomal Doxorubicin as KS-directed therapy with Rituximab has resulted in control of KICS. Rituximab and hyaluronidase human injection for subcutaneous use may be substituted after the first full IV dose [KICS-1 footnote d]. Steroids should generally be avoided [KICS-1 footnote b].

Alternative KS-directed systemic therapies (e.g., those listed for relapsed/refractory KS) plus Rituximab may be considered. In mild cases, KS-directed therapy alone may be sufficient, and Rituximab may be added later if inadequate response. Because flares of KS may occur due to prolonged Rituximab effect, KS-directed therapy should be continued for at least 3 months after cessation of Rituximab [KICS-1 footnote f].

regimens.guideline_category: Useful in certain circumstances for refractory KICS
regimens.notes: Tocilizumab blocks the IL-6 receptor but not the portion bound by viral IL-6. It may be added to KS-directed therapy for refractory KICS [KICS-1 footnote e].

regimens.guideline_category: Useful in certain circumstances for refractory KICS
regimens.notes: Siltuximab binds to human IL-6, preventing it from activating the IL-6 receptor. It may be added to KS-directed therapy for refractory KICS [KICS-1 footnote e].

regimens.setting: First-line for symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome limited cutaneous disease

drugs.name: Alitretinoin 0.1% gel
drugs.schedule: apply 2 times daily to affected skin sites; increase to 3–4 times daily if tolerated

trial_data.or_other_data: Cutaneous tumor response rate 37% for alitretinoin vs 7% for vehicle [74]

regimens.notes: Alitretinoin gel is a retinoid option for limited cutaneous Kaposi sarcoma. It was well tolerated with mostly mild to moderate adverse events limited to the application site [74,75].

regimens.setting: First-line for symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome limited cutaneous disease

drugs.name: Imiquimod 5% cream
drugs.schedule: apply one sachet to up to 20 cm2 of affected skin and cover with occlusive dressing for 8 hours three times per week; titrate frequency to effect and tolerability (up to once daily)

trial_data.trial_name: Phase I to II open-label trial
trial_data.or_other_data: Response rate 47% in HIV-negative patients [88]

regimens.notes: Imiquimod is a topical immune response modulator. Evidence is primarily from classic and transplant-associated Kaposi sarcoma; extrapolated to HIV-related KS based on expert opinion [82,88,89].

regimens.setting: First-line for symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome limited cutaneous disease

trial_data.or_other_data: CR 74%, PR 26% of lesions; recurrence rate 26% [104]

regimens.notes: Intralesional Vinblastine is effective for oral and cutaneous Kaposi sarcoma. Pain from injection is common and may persist for several days; NSAIDs may be useful. Use with caution on plantar and palmar surfaces [101-107].

regimens.setting: First-line for symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome limited cutaneous disease

drugs.name: Bleomycin
drugs.schedule: 0.5 unit per 0.5 cm2 of lesion every 3 to 4 weeks until response

trial_data.or_other_data: CR 43%, PR 44% of lesions [113]

regimens.notes: Intralesional Bleomycin has activity in cutaneous Kaposi sarcoma. Local pain may occur [111-113].

regimens.setting: First-line for symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome limited cutaneous disease

trial_data.or_other_data: CR without recurrence in 63% of patients [120]

regimens.notes: Cryotherapy should be done by clinicians with expertise in cutaneous cancer cryotherapy. It is most effective for non-nodular, smaller lesions (<1 cm) [87,120].

regimens.setting: First-line for symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome limited cutaneous disease
regimens.notes: Marginal excision is an option for local control of small lesions. Wide excision is not indicated as it does not contribute to control of Kaposi sarcoma [114-118].

regimens.setting: First-line for symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome limited cutaneous disease
regimens.notes: Electrodesiccation and curettage is an option for local control of small, symptomatic lesions. Data are extremely limited [119].

regimens.site: Kaposi sarcoma (limited cutaneous or advanced disease when systemic therapy not feasible)
regimens.notes: Radiation therapy options include various fractionation schemas: 6–8 Gy in 1 fraction, 20 Gy in 5 fractions, 24 Gy in 12 fractions, 30 Gy in 10–15 fractions, 40 Gy in 20 fractions. Lower doses for smaller superficial lesions; higher doses for extensive deep lesions. Caution with pre-existing lymphedema. For advanced disease, systemic therapy is preferred over RT [KS-E references 1-5].
